questions.q: In Hindu tradition, which natural element is the deity Agni the god of?
questions.explanation: Agni is the Vedic god of fire, especially the sacred fire of the sacrifice (yajna).

questions.q: In which body of scripture is Agni one of the most frequently invoked deities?
questions.explanation: Agni is among the most invoked deities of the Rigveda, second only to Indra in the number of hymns addressed to him.

questions.q: In the Vedic ritual, what does the fire-god Agni carry from mortals up to the gods?
questions.explanation: Agni is the fire of the yajna who carries the oblations offered into the flames up to the gods.

questions.q: Because he conveys offerings between humans and the gods, what role is the fire-god Agni often described as filling?
options: A messenger between mortals and the heavens
questions.explanation: As the fire that bears oblations skyward, Agni serves as the priest and messenger between mortals and the heavens.

questions.q: Which animal is traditionally depicted as the vahana (mount) of the fire-god Agni?
questions.explanation: Agni is depicted riding a ram (mesha) as his vahana.

questions.q: The fire-god Agni is famously called the mouth of whom?
questions.explanation: Because offerings placed in the sacred fire reach the deities, Agni is named the mouth of the gods.

questions.q: In Vedic iconography, the flames of the fire-god Agni are often counted as how many tongues?
questions.explanation: Agni is traditionally depicted with seven tongues of flame.

questions.q: How is the fire-god Agni said to be born anew at each kindling?
questions.explanation: Agni is said to be born anew from the kindling sticks (arani) whenever the ritual fire is freshly lit.

questions.q: The central sacred rite over which the fire-god Agni presides is known by which name?
questions.explanation: Agni is the fire of the yajna, the Vedic sacrificial offering.

questions.q: In classical iconography, the fire-god Agni is often shown with how many faces?
questions.explanation: Agni is commonly depicted with two faces.

questions.q: The fire-god Agni presides over which of the cardinal or intermediate directions as a dikpala (guardian of the directions)?
questions.explanation: Among the dikpalas, Agni is the guardian of the south-east direction (agneya).

questions.q: In Vedic hymns, the fire-god Agni is said to dwell in three forms across which three realms?
questions.explanation: Agni is described as threefold, present as fire on earth, as lightning in the atmosphere, and as the sun in heaven.

questions.q: In the Mahabharata, the fire-god Agni sought to consume which great forest with the aid of Krishna and Arjuna?
questions.explanation: In the Mahabharata, Agni consumes the Khandava forest with the help of Krishna and Arjuna, who hold back the gods seeking to extinguish him.

questions.q: During the burning of the Khandava forest in the Mahabharata, what did the fire-god Agni gift to Arjuna?
questions.explanation: In gratitude for help in consuming the Khandava forest, Agni gave Arjuna the celestial bow Gandiva.

questions.q: In the Ramayana, which heroine entered the sacred fire of Agni to demonstrate her purity in the agni pariksha?
questions.explanation: In the Ramayana, Sita undergoes the agni pariksha, entering Agni's fire, from which she emerges unharmed.

questions.q: In Hindu wedding tradition, the fire-god Agni serves as the divine witness to which central rite?
options: The circumambulation of the sacred fire (saptapadi/pheras)
The tying of a thread on the wrist
questions.explanation: Agni is the witness of the marriage, as the couple circumambulate the sacred fire in the saptapadi or seven steps.

questions.q: The fire-god Agni is closely paired with which other prominent Vedic deity, the two being among the most invoked gods of the Rigveda?
questions.explanation: Agni and Indra are the two most frequently invoked deities of the Rigveda and are often praised together.

questions.q: Within the framework of the Vedic sacrifice, the fire-god Agni is regarded as the divine version of which class of ritual specialist?
questions.explanation: Agni is hailed as the divine priest, the hotr who summons the gods and presents the offerings.

questions.q: In Vedic identification, the fire-god Agni is said to manifest in the sky as which celestial form?
questions.explanation: In his threefold nature, Agni is identified with the sun as his heavenly form.

questions.q: Which sacred draught or fluid, poured into the fire, is among the offerings the fire-god Agni receives in the yajna?
questions.explanation: Ghee, the clarified butter poured as oblation, is a principal offering received by Agni in the sacrifice.

questions.q: In Puranic accounts, the war-god Kartikeya (Skanda) is said to have been nurtured by the seed or fire connected with which deity, an aspect linked to Agni?
questions.explanation: Kartikeya was born of Shiva's fiery seed, which Agni and the Krittikas helped to bear and nurture in the Puranic accounts.

questions.q: What is the name of the offering, cast into Agni's fire and accompanied by the call svaha, in the Vedic ritual?
questions.explanation: The ahuti is the oblation poured into the fire with the exclamation svaha, which Agni carries to the gods.

questions.q: In several Vedic and Puranic genealogies, the wife of the fire-god Agni is named as which goddess?
questions.explanation: Svaha, the personification of the ritual exclamation, is named as the consort of Agni in Vedic and Puranic tradition.

questions.q: The fire-god Agni is counted among which group of Vedic deities associated with light and the divine?
questions.explanation: Agni is reckoned among the eight Vasus, the Vedic deities of natural phenomena and elemental abodes.

questions.q: The opening hymn of the Rigveda, its very first verse, is addressed to which deity?
questions.explanation: The first hymn of the Rigveda begins agnim ile, an invocation of Agni, the fire-god.

questions.q: According to a well-known Vedic and Puranic account, the fire-god Agni once hid from the gods within which element to escape his duties?
questions.explanation: In the tradition, Agni concealed himself within the waters and plants, and the gods sought him out to restore him to the sacrifice.

questions.q: In the Mahabharata episode of the Khandava forest, whose prolonged sacrifice is said to have left the fire-god Agni ailing, so that consuming the forest became his remedy?
questions.explanation: In the Mahabharata, Agni's prolonged sacrifice for King Shvetaki left him sated and ailing, and consuming the Khandava forest was his cure.

questions.q: An epithet of the fire-god Agni, Jatavedas, is traditionally understood to mean which of the following?
options: He who knows all beings or all that is born
questions.explanation: The epithet Jatavedas is understood to mean the one who knows all created beings, or who possesses knowledge of all that is born.

questions.q: In the Kena Upanishad, the fire-god Agni is humbled when he cannot perform which simple feat before the mysterious presence of Brahman?
options: Burning a single blade of grass
questions.explanation: In the Kena Upanishad, Agni boasts he can burn anything, yet cannot consume a single blade of grass, learning his power derives from Brahman.

questions.q: The cremation rite, in which the fire-god Agni receives the body of the deceased, is referred to by which term as the final sacrifice?
questions.explanation: The antyeshti, or final sacrifice, is the cremation rite in which the body is offered to Agni's fire.
